source_metadata.title_raw: Jose M. Sanchez - A-19-137 - August 8, 2019 - Sacramento

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Jose M. Sanchez 
Livingston City Attorney 
Meyers Nave 
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 1200 
Sacramento, California 95814 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-137 
Dear Mr. Sanchez: 
August 8, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Livingston City Councilmember 
Raul Garcia regarding Government Code Section 1090. 1 Please note that we are only providing 
advice under Section 1090 and not under any other conflict of interest laws. Also note that we are 
not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice 
we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts 
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
In regard to ou·r advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to it to the Office of the Attorney General and the Merced County District 
Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We are also required to specify 
whether we have received a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We 
received a written response from the Merced County District Attorney's Office, reviewed and 
considered that response in preparing this advice, and a copy of the response is attached to this 
letter. Finally, we are required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following 
advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." 
(Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in making, or 
the Livingston City Council from entering into, a Tax Sharing Agreement with County of Merced 
given that the Councilmember is employed by the County as a Deputy Sheriff? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in 
making, or the City Council from entering into, a Tax Sharing Agreement with the County because 
the noninterest exception set forth in Section 1091.5(a)(9) applies, so long as the Councilmember 
discloses his interest in the County at the time the City Council considers the Agreement, ~nd the 
interest is noted in the City Council's official records. 
1 All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-137 
Page No. 2 
Revenue and Taxation Code Section 99 requires a city seeking to annex real property to its 
incorporated territory, and a county affected by the potential annexation, to agree upon an exchange 
of property taxes which are derived from the real property that would be annexed by the city. For 
several years, the City of Livingston has been involved in discussions with County of Merced 
regarding the establishment of a Tax Sharing Agreement between the City and the County pursuant 
to Revenue and Taxation Code Section 99. 
You are the City Attorney for the City of Livingston and the authorized representative of 
Livingston City Councilmember Raul Garcia. The Councilmember was elected to the City Council 
in November 2018, and has been employed by the County as a Deputy Sheriff since June 2008. The 
Councilmember does not serve in a managerial role at the Sheriffs Department, is not involved in 
the County's internal discussions involving the Tax Sharing Agreement, and has not been involved 
in any City discussions related to the Agreement to date. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 generally prohibits a public officer or employee from making or participating 
in the making of a contract in which he or she is financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned 
with financial interests, other than remote interests and noninterests, that prevent a public officer or 
employee from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests 
of his or her agency. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) A contract made in violation of 
Section 1090 is void (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633,646), and an official with a prohibited 
interest in such a contract is subject to civil penalties (Sections 1097.1 and 1097.3), as well as 
criminal penalties if the violation was willful (Section I 097). 
Under Section 1090, a member of a public agency's governing body is conclusively 
presumed to participate.in the making of a contract under the governing body's authority, 
irrespective of whether he or she actually participates in the making of that contract. (Thomson v. 
Call, supra, at pp. 649-650.) Therefore, Section 1090 prohibits the entire body from entering into a 
contract in which a member of the body is financially interested, even if that member abstains from 
participating in the making of the contract-or fully discloses his or her financial interest in the 
contract. (Ibid.) 
Because Section 1090 applies to city councilmembers (id. at p. 633), the Councilmember is 
subject to Section 1090. The decisions at issue involve a contract, the Tax Sharing Agreement 
between the City and the County. As noted above, the Councilmember would be conclusively 
presumed to participate in the making of the Agreement because the City Council is authorized to 
enter into that contract. The Councilmember would have a potential interest in that contract because 
he is a County employee. (See Section 1091.5(a)(9).) Therefore, Section I 090 would prohibit the 
Councilmember from making or participating in making, and the City Council from entering into, 
the Agreement unless a remote-interest or noninterest exception or the rule of necessity applies. 


File No. A-19-137 
Page No. 3 
The Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where 
the financial interest involved is deemed to be a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a 
"noninterest," as defined in Section 1091. 5. Most pertinent to the facts presented, Section 
1091.5(a)(9) provides that an officer or employee has a noninterest in a contract if his or her interest 
in that contract is: 
(9) That of a person receiving salary, per diem, or reimbursement for 
expenses from a government entity, unless the contract directly involves the-
department of the government entity that employs the officer or employee, 
provided that the interest is disclosed to the body or board at the time of 
consideration of the contract, and provided further that the interest is noted in 
its official record. 
In Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1081, the California Supreme Court 
determined that this exception applies when "the contract involves no direct financial gain, does not 
directly affect the official's employing department, and is only with the general government entity 
for which the official works." The Court went on to state that "Section 1091.5(a)(9) excuses 
conflicts that arise from the identity of the party with or on whose behalf one is contracting (one's 
employer, other than one's own specific department), but not conflicts that arise from the actual 
terms of the contract." (Ibid.) 
Thus, an officer or employee has a noninterest in his or her government-entity employer 
under Section 1091.5(a)(9) if: (1) the contract at issue involves no direct financial gain to the 
official or employee; (2) the contract does not directly affect the official's or employee's 
department; (3) the interest is disclosed to the body or board at the time the contract is considered; 
and ( 4) the interest is noted in the body or board's official record. 
The Councilmember works as a Deputy Sheriff in the Merced County Sheriffs Department. 
The facts presented provide no indication that his compensation from the County for being a 
Deputy Sheriff is in any manner contingent upon or related to any relationship between the City and 
the County. He does not serve in a managerial role with the Sheriffs Department, and is not 
involved in any of the County's internal discussions involving the Tax Sharing Agreement. And the 
facts presented also provide no indication that the Agreement would result in a direct financial gain 
to the Councilmember or directly affect the Sheriffs Department. 
Therefore, Section 1090 would not prohibit the Councilmember from making or 
participating in making, or the City Council from entering into, the Tax Sharing Agreement 
between the City and the County because the noninterest exception of Section 1091.5(a)(9) applies, 
so long as the Councilmember discloses his interest in the County to the City Council at the time 
the Agreement is considered, and the interest is noted in the City Council's official record. 


File No. A-19-137 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questio11s on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:aja 
Attachment Enclosed 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

1

If you have other questio11s on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
